source_texts: 하고 딴전을 부리며 서녘 하늘을 쳐다본다.
볕에 그을어 이글이글하게 타는 듯하던 얼굴과 그 건장한 몸뚱이를 기울어 가는 창백한 달빛이 씻어내린다. 파르스름한 액체와 같은 달빛이.

영신은 다시 무슨 생각에 잠겨 동혁의 커다란 그림자가 저의 눈앞에 가로 비친 것을 들여다보고 잠자코 있다. 조금 전까지도 외로움과 쓸쓸함을 못 견디어 바람모지에 외따로 선 나무처럼 바들바들 떨고 있던 영신은, 동혁이가 와서 제 곁에 턱 앉은 것이 큰 바위 속에다가 뿌리를 박은 것만치나 신변이 든든한 것을 느꼈다. 그와 동시에 애상적이던 기분은 구름과 같이 흩어지고 안개처럼 스러졌다. 다만 동혁의 윤곽만이 점점 뚜렷하게 커져서 제 몸이 그 그늘 속으로 차츰차츰 기어들어가는 것 같은 환각을 느낄 따름이다.
한참 만에 동혁은 무겁게 입을 열었다.

Shift to 'communal paddy' project: leasing land, saving profits for a meeting hall, philosophy of unity through shared labor. Scene moves to rice paddy; members plow with oxen. Donghyeok/Yeongsin discuss intellectuals enduring physical toil. Sharp contrast drawn: Baek's urban refinement vs. Chilryong's leech-bitten, mud-stained legs.
  - *Hardship & Class Friction (pp. 61–70)*: Intensive labor (harrowing, manuring, leveling). Sparse communal lunch (barley/millet) contrasts with Donghyeok family's slightly better meal (sedum kimchi). Geonbae details farming cycle/economic despair (cost of vices). Donghwa mocks Gimman (Pastor Kang's son), a neurotic Tokyo returnee observing with detached arrogance. Gimman interacts condescendingly; Yeongsin, uncomfortable, withdraws with Geonbae to discuss class structures ('yangban' vs. commoner).
  - *Organization & Escalating Conflict (pp. 71–80)*: Yeongsin joins calisthenics despite Donghyeok's warnings, sparking gossip that ironically boosts attendance. She delivers passionate speeches, organizing 'Hangok-ri Women's Labor Association' (literacy, micro-savings) with Geonbae's wife. Gimman, rejected, escalates harassment via servants/letters; brother Gicheon (usurer/council candidate) actively opposes movement. Donghyeok intercepts invitations to protect Yeongsin. Ends with Donghyeok's mother treating Yeongsin as daughter-in-law; couple maintains strict formal distance under constant surveillance.

She had never expected any shining furnishings in such a humble household, especially for a bachelor, yet the room was far too austere for someone who had attended professional school. The earthen floor was covered only with a rush mat; there was not a single item that caught the eye. On the desk placed at the head of the room stood a few notebooks he had used while attending school, along with scattered newspapers and magazines. At the foot of the room lay a single bedding set, rolled up as if kicked into place. Indeed, there was one thing she had overlooked: an earthenware oil lamp attached to the wall papered with cardboard, which was no different from those she had seen in other farmhouses while passing by today.
